bosch-dc-microgrid-california-honda-faci.summary: California Energy Commission grant EPC-14-053 funded Bosch's 380 V direct-current building microgrid demonstration at an American Honda Motor Co. warehouse in Chino, California. The final installation coupled 286 kW of rooftop solar, a 180 kW/540 kWh lithium-ion battery, Bosch energy management, DC lighting, fan drives, and forklift charging on a common DC bus. CEC's 2024 retrospective says the system was removed after the grant because Bosch discontinued the commercial offering.
bosch-dc-microgrid-california-honda-faci.status: Demonstration completed in 2019 and subsequently uninstalled; CEC reported in 2024 that Bosch discontinued the DC Building Microgrid Platform offering.

organizations.name: Robert Bosch LLC
organizations.role: CEC grantee, system integrator, and supplier of the DC microgrid energy-management system

organizations.name: American Honda Motor Co.
organizations.role: Host and customer at the Chino parts-distribution warehouse

organizations.name: California Energy Commission
organizations.role: EPIC funder and project administrator under agreement EPC-14-053

organizations.name: UC Davis California Lighting Technology Center
organizations.role: Design, training, and performance partner for the DC lighting and building-load demonstration

organizations.name: Navigant Consulting
organizations.role: Design and independent performance-validation partner named at award

organizations.name: Maxwell Technologies
organizations.role: Supply-chain project partner

organizations.name: Imergy Power Systems
organizations.role: Original supply-chain project partner; the planned flow-battery path was disrupted when the vendor went out of business

organizations.name: Johnson Controls
organizations.role: Supplier of the lithium-ion energy-storage container used in the completed demonstration

specifications.label: Rooftop photovoltaic capacity
specifications.value: 286 kW DC

specifications.label: Battery rating
specifications.value: 180 kW / 540 kWh lithium-ion (3-hour duration)

specifications.label: DC distribution voltage
specifications.value: 380 V DC common bus

specifications.label: Energy mix
specifications.value: On-site solar plus lithium-ion storage and utility-grid supply; no fuel-fired generator was part of the final DC microgrid

specifications.label: CEC award and match
specifications.value: $2,817,566 EPIC award; $2,389,216 spent; $1,797,544 match funding

specifications.label: CEC project term
specifications.value: June 30, 2015 to March 29, 2019; an older PNNL tracker listed March 29, 2018 as the planned end

equipment.detail: DC microgrid energy-management system coordinating sources, storage, and DC loads
Containerized 180 kW/540 kWh lithium-ion system connected to the DC bus
High-bay LED lighting, industrial-fan variable-frequency drives, and forklift charging operated on the DC architecture

technicalDetails.detail: The building-scale system avoided repeated DC-to-AC-to-DC conversions by linking rooftop PV, battery storage, and compatible building loads on a 380 V DC bus while retaining utility service.

CEC reported 2-6% DC-load efficiency improvement, 7-10% greater solar utilization, and an 8% storage-efficiency improvement; the 2019 EPIC summary reported 3-5% overall efficiency improvement for lighting, ventilation, and forklift charging.
The system supported designated critical loads during outage testing and avoided use of the site's diesel backup generator.

CEC estimated roughly 30% lower total cost of ownership at commercial scale; the host facility manager estimated 45-50% lower maintenance cost, but CEC cautioned that project delays left limited baseline and operating data.

Flow-battery supplier failure, interconnection delays, a separately managed rooftop-solar project, and rate changes delayed the work. After the grant, Bosch discontinued the product offering and the demonstration equipment was uninstalled, so the project did not proceed to a commercial launch.
Current CEC and PNNL project records place the host warehouse in Chino, California, not Rancho Cordova.

brevoort-co-op-microgrid.summary: The Brevoort cooperative at 11 Fifth Avenue in Manhattan installed a 400 kW natural-gas combined-heat-and-power microgrid built from four 100 kW Tecogen InVerde modules. Commissioned in 2010, it normally operated in parallel with Con Edison under zero-export control and islanded for six days after Hurricane Sandy, serving electricity, heat, domestic hot water, elevators, and other building loads.
brevoort-co-op-microgrid.status: Commissioned in 2010 and documented operating through at least 2013; no reliable public source confirms the system's operating condition through 2026.

organizations.name: The Brevoort cooperative
organizations.role: Owner and operator of the 20-story residential building and its CHP microgrid

organizations.name: Tecogen
organizations.role: Supplier of the four InVerde CHP modules, inverter controls, and microgrid operating technology

organizations.name: Energy Concepts
organizations.role: Design engineer for the CHP/microgrid installation

organizations.name: Con Edison
organizations.role: Host electric utility; the system normally paralleled the utility and maintained a minimum import

organizations.name: Wisconsin Alumni Research Foundation
organizations.role: Licensor of CERTS autonomous load-sharing software used by Tecogen

specifications.label: CHP electrical capacity
specifications.value: 400 kW nominal: four 100 kW modules, each capable of 125 kW peak

specifications.label: Absorption chiller
specifications.value: 90 refrigeration tons

specifications.label: Energy mix
specifications.value: Natural-gas CHP supplies electricity and recovered heat; Con Edison supplies supplemental grid electricity. No solar or battery storage is documented.

specifications.label: Building load profile
specifications.value: Winter average/peak about 250/350 kW; summer average/peak about 400/550 kW

specifications.label: Installed project cost
specifications.value: $3.2 million

equipment.detail: Four natural-gas engine-generator modules rated 100 kW each, with heat recovery from engine jacket water and exhaust
90-ton absorption chiller using recovered CHP heat
Tecogen inverter controls with CERTS autonomous power sharing and a utility-protection panel for isolation and reconnection

technicalDetails.detail: In normal grid-parallel operation the controls maintained at least 50 kW of utility import and prohibited export; typically three engines ran in winter and all four in summer.

UL 1741-certified CERTS logic let modules share load autonomously without a master controller. A utility-protection panel detected grid failure, opened the service breaker, and initiated an island restart.

The automatic utility breaker did not operate during Sandy, so the superintendent manually isolated the building and restarted the CHP. The building then ran islanded for six days, supporting residents and temporary guests with electricity, heat, water, and elevators.
The IEEE performance paper calculated as much as 92% total fuel utilization when all recoverable heat was used.
The IEEE analysis calculated $228,871 of 2013 energy savings on a 2007-cost basis and expected payback under five years; EPA reported approximately $300,000 annual realized savings.
This record is the Brevoort at 11 Fifth Avenue and should not be combined with the separate Brevoort East cooperative project, which public databases list with different CHP capacity.

sources.title: Grid-Independent Cogen System from Tecogen Comes Through During Hurricane Sandy
The Brevoort: 1950s Greenwich Village Co-Op Retrofitted with 21st Century Green Technology

brookhaven-microgrid-feasibility-study.summary: Brookhaven's Independence Hill Microgrid was a NY Prize Stage 1 feasibility design linking Brookhaven Town Hall and Sachem High School East on Long Island. The preferred concept combined two new natural-gas CHP plants, two existing 10 kW wind turbines, existing diesel backup, and a proposed battery over an underground interconnection. The study did not establish that the project was built.
brookhaven-microgrid-feasibility-study.status: Stage 1 feasibility study completed in 2016. The Independence Hill project is absent from NYSERDA's published complete list of Stage 2 awardees, indicating it did not advance in NY Prize; no construction or commissioning evidence was found through 2026.

organizations.name: Town of Brookhaven
organizations.role: NY Prize applicant, Town Hall site host, and proposed microgrid owner/operator

organizations.name: Sachem Central School District
organizations.role: Host and critical-load customer at Sachem High School East; considered as a possible co-owner

organizations.name: ERS
organizations.role: Project proposer and lead feasibility/design consultant

organizations.name: Schneider Electric
organizations.role: Developed the proposed microgrid control architecture and supporting cost estimates with ERS

organizations.name: Brookhaven National Laboratory
organizations.role: Provided DER-CAM optimization, economic modeling, and software support

organizations.name: New York State Energy Research and Development Authority
organizations.role: NY Prize Stage 1 funder and program administrator

organizations.name: PSEG Long Island
organizations.role: Serving utility and prospective interconnection/ownership participant considered in the study

specifications.label: Proposed Town Hall CHP
specifications.value: 1,148 kW natural-gas combined heat and power

specifications.label: Proposed Sachem High School CHP
specifications.value: 383 kW natural-gas combined heat and power

specifications.label: Existing wind generation
specifications.value: Two 10 kW turbines, one at each site (20 kW total)

specifications.label: Existing diesel backup
specifications.value: 300 kW at Town Hall; the report lists the school generator as 500 kW in the main resource table but 300 kW in a later DER table

specifications.label: Proposed battery power
specifications.value: 50-150 kW; energy capacity and final technology were not selected

specifications.label: Proposed energy mix
specifications.value: Natural-gas CHP, existing wind, existing diesel for outages, battery storage, and utility supply; photovoltaic and wastewater-methane generation remained future options with capacity TBD

specifications.label: Feasibility-stage capital estimate
specifications.value: $9,649,136 installed capital plus $611,000 planning/design, approximately $10.26 million total

equipment.detail: Two natural-gas CHP packages totaling 1,531 kW; manufacturer and model were not selected in Stage 1
One 10 kW wind turbine at Town Hall and one 10 kW wind turbine at Sachem High School East
Feasibility-level supervisory control architecture for grid-parallel, island, load-shed, and resynchronization functions; final product/model not selected

technicalDetails.detail: The two facilities would be joined by an underground distribution cable and normally operate grid-connected without exporting power. Controls were intended to detect outages, island automatically, prioritize renewable generation, shed noncritical loads, black-start, and reconnect after utility restoration.
The study modeled annual CHP output of 5,608 MWh at Town Hall and 3,350 MWh at the school, plus 22 MWh per year from each wind turbine. Diesel generation was reserved for outage operation.

The preferred design was modeled to cover 98% of annual electric needs and assumed seven days of natural-gas availability during a major outage; these were feasibility assumptions, not measured results.
The study projected $810,352 in annual energy savings, approximately $140,000 annual operating and maintenance cost, and about a 15-year simple payback.

The feasibility model reduced annual carbon dioxide at both sites but increased NOx and particulate emissions under its uncontrolled natural-gas CHP assumptions, illustrating that the proposal was not an all-renewable design.
Tecumseh Elementary School was evaluated initially and then removed because the district planned to close it; the final study covered Town Hall and Sachem High School East only.

NYSERDA describes its published Stage 2 list as complete, and Independence Hill is not present. The conclusion that it stopped after Stage 1 is therefore a program-record inference rather than an explicit cancellation notice.

sources.title: NY Prize Stage 1 Feasibility Assessment: Independence Hill Microgrid

brooklyn-microgrid.summary: Brooklyn Microgrid was LO3 Energy's peer-to-peer local-energy trading pilot in Brooklyn, New York. Participants used custom meters and the Exergy blockchain platform to transact attributes associated with rooftop solar while electricity continued to flow over Con Edison's distribution system. A physical ten-block islandable microgrid was discussed, but public evidence documents a virtual marketplace pilot rather than a completed electrical island.
brooklyn-microgrid.status: Pilot no longer operating by 2025. Public activity is documented from the first 2016 trades through a 2019 regulatory-sandbox effort; the project site stopped updating in 2019 and LO3's site later disappeared.

organizations.name: LO3 Energy
organizations.role: Project developer and operator; built the Exergy transactive-energy platform and custom participant metering

organizations.name: Siemens
organizations.role: Technology collaborator and investor providing grid-management and data-analytics expertise; physical microgrid integration remained planned

organizations.name: ConsenSys
organizations.role: Co-developer with LO3 of the original TransActive Grid blockchain demonstration

organizations.name: Con Edison
organizations.role: Distribution utility whose wires continued to carry participants' physical electricity

organizations.name: Brooklyn community participants
organizations.role: Prosumers and consumers using the pilot marketplace; LO3 described a 50/50 community-and-company ownership concept

specifications.label: Documented participant scale
specifications.value: More than 60 community members by December 2017; later reports described about 50-60 solar prosumers and roughly 500-750 participants or sign-ups, reflecting different dates and definitions

specifications.label: Energy mix
specifications.value: Participating rooftop solar attributes plus ordinary Con Edison grid electricity; no verified central generation or storage capacity

specifications.label: Planned physical footprint
specifications.value: A ten-block islandable microgrid serving community solar and critical facilities was proposed, but not shown in public sources to have been constructed

specifications.label: 2019 regulatory sandbox
specifications.value: Approved as a 12-month pilot anticipated to enroll about 200 participants trading energy attributes

equipment.detail: Decentralized-ledger marketplace matching local bids and offers and tracking energy attributes
Custom smart meter/computer packages recorded participant production and consumption for the blockchain marketplace
Microgrid-management algorithms and grid-control integration were collaboration goals; LO3 said this physical integration had not yet been done in Brooklyn

technicalDetails.detail: Users could bid according to price, fuel source, and other energy attributes through an application. Early transactions represented local solar value while financial settlement and actual power delivery still depended on conventional systems and Con Edison wires.

The demonstrated system was a virtual peer-to-peer marketplace. Islanding a ten-block section to support a high school, medical facilities, and other critical loads was a future plan, not a documented completed capability.
New York rules did not generally permit non-utilities to sell electricity directly. The 2019 sandbox therefore focused on trading energy attributes while regulators evaluated a broader framework.

A 2025 Electrosuisse follow-up found the project had quietly ended: brooklyn.energy had not been updated since 2019, LO3's website was gone, and an early participant confirmed the marketplace was no longer operating. His solar array remained an ordinary Con Edison-interconnected system.
The first demonstration was also called TransActive Grid; later public materials used Brooklyn Microgrid and BMG.

burlington-dc-microgrid.summary: ARDA Power's industrial DC microgrid demonstration in Burlington, Ontario, used a bipolar 380 V DC platform to integrate 16 kW of solar, a 10 kW natural-gas generator, a 20 kW/100 kWh vanadium-redox flow battery, grid service, and DC/AC loads. The battery converter regulated the DC bus while distributed source and load controls coordinated without relying on a central real-time communications link.
burlington-dc-microgrid.status: Demonstration commissioned around 2017. ARDA still lists the Burlington C&I deployment on its project page, but no public source verifies current operation or performance through 2026.

organizations.name: ARDA Power
organizations.role: Microgrid developer, integrator, and supplier of the battery-centric DC platform and power converters

organizations.name: Gildemeister Energy Storage GmbH
organizations.role: Supplier of the CellCube vanadium-redox flow battery and interface-development partner

organizations.name: Etratech
organizations.role: Burlington industrial host identified in project literature

organizations.name: Sustainable Development Technology Canada
organizations.role: Provided a $400,000 grant for the DC microgrid demonstration

organizations.name: Gentherm
organizations.role: Acquired host company Etratech in November 2017 and continued operations at its Burlington headquarters

specifications.label: Photovoltaic capacity
specifications.value: 16 kW total, configured as two 8 kW arrays

specifications.label: Flow-battery rating
specifications.value: 20 kW / 100 kWh vanadium-redox flow battery (5-hour duration)

specifications.label: Natural-gas generator
specifications.value: 10 kW

specifications.label: DC bus
specifications.value: Bipolar +/-380 V DC

specifications.label: Energy mix
specifications.value: 16 kW solar as the renewable source, 10 kW natural gas for dispatchable backup, utility-grid supply, and 20 kW/100 kWh flow-battery storage

equipment.detail: 20 kW/100 kWh vanadium-redox flow-battery system
Bidirectional DC/DC converter that exclusively regulates DC-bus voltage in the battery-centric architecture
Unidirectional MPPT DC/DC interfaces for the two photovoltaic arrays
A 230 W LED fixture replacing a 400 W metal-halide fixture, an 18,000 BTU air conditioner, and a 48 V mini-refrigerator were among documented controllable loads

technicalDetails.detail: The battery energy-storage converter forms and regulates the DC bus. Sources and loads respond locally to bus voltage, allowing real-time coordination without communications; supervisory communications are used for optimization, monitoring, and data collection.
ARDA describes the platform as capable of seamless transition between grid-connected and islanded operation, with the battery remaining the bus-voltage reference in both modes.
A PLC could shed or dim loads based on system conditions while bidirectional interfaces supported AC and DC loads on the common bus.

Gentherm acquired Etratech in November 2017 and said it would continue operations at the Burlington headquarters; this establishes a host ownership change, not the microgrid's current operating status.

camp-pendleton-microgrid.summary: The assigned Camp Pendleton record is the Area 52 School of Infantry FractalGrid demonstration, not later Camp Pendleton microgrids. Four distributed microgrids combined fixed and concentrated solar, diesel backup, flywheel and GEL-battery storage, vehicle-to-grid capability, and CleanSpark controls across a 12 kV system that could coordinate as a roughly 1.1 MW-class site microgrid.
camp-pendleton-microgrid.status: Area 52 was commissioned in 2014 and CleanSpark reported continuous operation for more than three years in 2017. CleanSpark expected release to the Marine Corps in 2018, but no public source verifies the final transfer or continued operation through 2026.

organizations.name: U.S. Marine Corps / Camp Pendleton
organizations.role: Host and intended ultimate recipient/operator of the Area 52 system

organizations.name: California Energy Commission
organizations.role: Provided a reported $1.7 million grant for the Area 52 demonstration

organizations.name: Harper Construction
organizations.role: CEC grantee and general contractor; later transferred the microgrid control infrastructure to CleanSpark

organizations.name: CleanSpark
organizations.role: Subcontracted designer, controls developer, integrator, installer, and later owner/operator under the transfer arrangement

organizations.name: Quantum Energy Storage
organizations.role: Flywheel energy-storage developer/supplier for the Area 52 demonstration

specifications.label: Area 52 photovoltaic resources
specifications.value: Approximately 525 kW total: 225 kW concentrated PV plus 300 kW flat-plate PV

specifications.label: Area 52 diesel backup
specifications.value: 200 kW

specifications.label: Area 52 storage design
specifications.value: 300 kWh total: 240 kWh flywheel storage plus 60 kWh deep-cycle GEL battery storage

specifications.label: Area 52 coordinated microgrid scale
specifications.value: Four local microgrids coordinated into a roughly 1.1 MW-class 12 kV system; 1.1 MW describes the combined site/grid scale, not installed generation alone

specifications.label: Area 52 energy mix
specifications.value: Solar PV plus flywheel/GEL storage and vehicle-to-grid support, with a 200 kW diesel generator for dispatchable backup and utility-grid service in normal operation

specifications.label: CEC project term
specifications.value: June 30, 2013 to March 31, 2015

equipment.detail: Distributed control platform coordinating four local microgrids, islanding, reconnection, generation, storage, and loads
Fifteen dual-axis concentrated-PV trackers totaling about 225 kW
Two low-speed flywheel systems; public sources describe a 240 kWh project allocation, while a contemporaneous technology report described a tested 60 kW/120 kWh unit
60 kWh deep-cycle GEL battery bank supporting the flywheel-based storage design

technicalDetails.detail: Distributed controls formed four separate microgrids at Area 52 and allowed them to align autonomously as a larger 12 kV microgrid, island from the utility, and reconnect after restoration.
CleanSpark reported in late 2017 that the mPulse system had operated continuously for more than three years with minimal maintenance.

CleanSpark bought the control infrastructure from Harper for $1 and assumed a ten-year operating/maintenance obligation. A 2018 filing said the company expected to release the assets to the Marine Corps that year, but that forward-looking statement is not proof the transfer closed.
The 1.1 MW figure in CleanSpark filings is the coordinated site microgrid scale and should not be added to the 525 kW solar and 200 kW diesel figures as another generator.
A separate Critical Infrastructure Support microgrid accepted in 2019 used about 150 kW solar and 400 kWh iron-flow storage. It is not the 2014 Area 52 FractalGrid represented by this record.

California announced a separate Haybarn Energy Reliability Center in December 2024: $42 million for an initial 6 MW/48 MWh battery system, expandable to 50 MW/400 MWh, with operation expected in summer 2027. This does not establish the current status of Area 52.

camp-smith-microgrid.summary: Camp H.M. Smith in Hawaii was the third and most installation-wide phase of the DoD/DOE/DHS SPIDERS demonstration. Completed in late 2015, it integrated the base's utility service, four new Tier 4 interim diesel generators, selected existing generators, a 300 kW photovoltaic array, strategically placed battery storage, secure supervisory controls, and fast-transfer power electronics to sustain critical Pacific Command loads.
camp-smith-microgrid.status: SPIDERS Phase III completed and successfully demonstrated in late 2015. Marine Corps Base Hawaii still lists the Camp Smith micro-grid as an energy innovation, but no public Camp Smith-specific operating or performance data through 2026 were found.

organizations.name: U.S. Pacific Command
organizations.role: Camp H.M. Smith host and mission operator for the installation-wide Phase III demonstration

organizations.name: U.S. Department of Defense
organizations.role: SPIDERS sponsor and transition customer

organizations.name: U.S. Department of Energy
organizations.role: SPIDERS sponsor and technical-program partner

organizations.role: SPIDERS co-sponsor focused on cybersecure critical infrastructure

organizations.role: Technical manager for the SPIDERS joint capability demonstration

organizations.name: Intelligent Power & Energy Research Corporation
organizations.role: Lead developer of the GridMaster microgrid controls, communications, and cybersecurity platform

organizations.name: Go Electric
organizations.role: Supplier of the instantaneous synchronization/islanding module used for sub-cycle transfer and generator synchronization

organizations.name: Hawaiian Electric Company
organizations.role: Serving utility and coordination partner for grid-parallel and island transition requirements

organizations.name: Sandia National Laboratories
organizations.role: Microgrid design, systems engineering, and cybersecurity partner

specifications.label: Installation scale
specifications.value: Approximately 5 MW class, covering essentially the whole Camp H.M. Smith installation because critical loads dominated base demand

specifications.label: Existing photovoltaic capacity
specifications.value: 300 kW

specifications.label: New dispatchable generation
specifications.value: Four Tier 4 interim stationary prime diesel generators; individual and aggregate ratings were not published in the reviewed sources

specifications.label: Battery storage
specifications.value: Strategically located storage supporting instantaneous transition, power quality, peak management, and generator start; public reviewed sources do not give the installed kW/kWh rating

specifications.label: Energy mix
specifications.value: Utility-grid electricity from Hawaiian Electric, 300 kW on-site solar, four new diesel generators plus selected existing diesel generation, and battery storage

equipment.detail: Secure supervisory control overlay integrating existing SCADA/PLCs, generator dispatch, renewable resources, storage, load shedding, and resynchronization
Instantaneous synchronization/islanding module maintaining load through transfers under one electrical cycle and synchronizing generators after outage detection
Four Tier 4 interim stationary diesel generators, supplemented by a selected subset of the base's existing generator fleet

technicalDetails.detail: Phase III demonstrated installation-scale islanding, black start, renewable integration, generator dispatch, power-factor correction, peak management, load shedding, and secure reconnection with Hawaiian Electric.

GridMaster used an enclave architecture over existing control assets and underwent national-laboratory vulnerability and red-team evaluation. It received a DoD Platform IT Risk Approval/Authorization to Operate in August 2015, reported as the first DoD microgrid-control cybersecurity accreditation.

DOE's final report states that all three SPIDERS phases successfully operated their microgrids and met the joint demonstration goals; it does not publish a Camp Smith-specific post-2015 fuel-savings data series.

The report suggests that roughly 30% fossil-fuel reduction during long outages could be a reasonable program expectation, but this was not presented as measured Camp Smith performance and should not be treated as such.
Marine Corps Base Hawaii's current resources page continues to identify the Camp Smith micro-grid among installation energy innovations, but provides no operating statistics or refurbishment history.

cannon-afb.summary: The historical Cannon AFB microgrid record corresponds to a FY2011 Sandia energy-surety conceptual design/assessment funded by DOE's Office of Electricity and the Department of Defense. Sandia's later portfolio places it among military conceptual studies under 1 MW, not among operational prototypes. No public design package, equipment selection, construction award, or commissioned microgrid was found.
cannon-afb.status: Conceptual assessment only in FY2011; no evidence it advanced to construction. Later Cannon solar-efficiency and hydrogen-backup projects are separate resilience efforts, not proof that the FY2011 microgrid was built.

organizations.name: Sandia National Laboratories
organizations.role: Performed the FY2011 military energy-surety microgrid conceptual design/assessment

organizations.name: U.S. Department of Energy Office of Electricity
organizations.role: Co-funder/sponsor identified for the FY2011 conceptual assessment

organizations.name: U.S. Department of Defense / U.S. Air Force
organizations.role: Co-sponsor and installation owner for the FY2011 assessment

organizations.name: Ameresco
organizations.role: Developer under a separate 2021 energy-savings performance contract for solar and facility controls

organizations.name: Defense Logistics Agency Energy
organizations.role: Contracting partner for the separate $18.9 million 2021 Cannon energy-savings performance contract

organizations.name: Prometheus Hydrogen
organizations.role: Designed, installed, operates, and maintains the separate 2026 HEGO solid-state hydrogen backup pilot

specifications.label: FY2011 conceptual microgrid scale
specifications.value: Categorized by Sandia as a military conceptual design/assessment under 1 MW; no more precise design capacity was published

specifications.label: FY2011 conceptual energy mix
specifications.value: Not publicly specified; no source-backed generator, storage, or renewable selection was found for the assessment

specifications.label: Separate 2021 ESPC photovoltaic capacity
specifications.value: 1.9 MW ground-mounted solar PV, planned to supply about 4% of base electricity

specifications.label: Separate 2021 ESPC value and savings
specifications.value: $18.9 million; projected $1.1 million first-year and $33 million performance-period savings

specifications.label: Separate 2021 ESPC projected impact
specifications.value: 77,883 million Btu annual energy reduction and 10,700 metric tons annual carbon reduction

specifications.label: Separate 2026 HEGO rating
specifications.value: Capacity not publicly disclosed

equipment.detail: No public source identifies selected generation, storage, switchgear, controller manufacturer, or equipment model for the conceptual assessment

A 1.9 MW ground-mounted photovoltaic array, transformer upgrades, direct-digital HVAC controls, and LED lighting were included in Ameresco's 2021 ESPC; no battery or islanding controller was announced
Compact solid-state hydrogen energy-production backup system deployed for the base water-supply system in June 2026

technicalDetails.detail: Sandia's 2017 portfolio explicitly lists Cannon AFB as a FY2011 DOE Office of Electricity/DoD conceptual design or assessment under 1 MW, while operational prototypes and large demonstrations are shown in separate categories.
A 2010 Sandia presentation listed Cannon among installations expressing FY2011 project interest; the later 2017 portfolio confirms an assessment occurred but provides no evidence of implementation.

The Air Force/DLA Energy awarded Ameresco an ESPC in June 2021 covering five conservation measures across 119 buildings and a 1.9 MW PV array. Announcements targeted Q1 2023 completion, but the reviewed official sources do not confirm final completion or describe the project as an islandable microgrid.

On June 4, 2026 the Air Force demonstrated Prometheus Hydrogen's HEGO backup system for Cannon's water supply. The solid-state hydrogen system is being evaluated as an alternative to mobile and stationary diesel backup generators; the Air Force calls it a pilot, not the FY2011 microgrid.

As of June 2026 Cannon still relied on mobile and stationary diesel generators for outage backup and was piloting hydrogen for one water-supply application. Public sources do not establish a base-wide islandable microgrid or storage fleet.
The FY2011 conceptual microgrid, the 2021 solar/controls ESPC, and the 2026 water-system hydrogen backup pilot are three distinct efforts and should not have their capacities or status merged.
